methods.content_preview:
Methods
Information interaction is another crucial technology for the
joint classification of HSI and LiDAR data, which enhances
the richness of feature representations and the discriminability
of classifiers. Typical techniques include image reconstruction,
contrastive learning, etc. As for the technique of reconstruction, it plays an important role in preserving the integrity of
the information, and many methods adopt this strategy. For
instance, Zhang et al. [43] proposed an interleaving perception
CNN network. It consists of a bidirectional autoencoder and a
two-branch CNN, which aim to reconstruct HSI and LiDAR
images and produce the final classification maps. Moreover,
two multimodal structural constraints are designed to ensure


the completeness of the fused features. Similarly, Wu et al.
[44] developed a cross-channel reconstruction network for
HSI and LiDAR joint classification. Unlike typical direct
reconstruction strategies, this model learns a more compact
fusion represen...

paragraphs.text:
Methods
Information interaction is another crucial technology for the
joint classification of HSI and LiDAR data, which enhances
the richness of feature representations and the discriminability
of classifiers. Typical techniques include image reconstruction,
contrastive learning, etc. As for the technique of reconstruction, it plays an important role in preserving the integrity of
the information, and many methods adopt this strategy. For
instance, Zhang et al. [43] proposed an interleaving perc...

the completeness of the fused features. Similarly, Wu et al.
[44] developed a cross-channel reconstruction network for
HSI and LiDAR joint classification. Unlike typical direct
reconstruction strategies, this model learns a more compact
fusion representation by reconstructing flipped features, which
benefits downstream classification. A coupled adversarial
learning-based classification (CALC) method was introduced
in [45] under the reconstructed framework. First, a game
construction is used for ...
Framework of the proposed MICF-Net. (a) Overall framework. (b) CMAFT. (c) SGFF module (taking the HSI branch as an example).

and a 2-D convolution with the kernel size of 1 Г— 1 are
stacked sequentially to extract spectral information from HSI.
Differently, since LiDAR is a single-channel image, two
1 Г— 1 2-D convolutions are stacked to extract elevation
information from LiDAR. Here, the number of filters of the
two convolutional layers in the HSI branch are 8 and 64,
respectively, while in the LiDAR branch, they are 32 and
64. Furthermore, to speed up the convergence of the model,
a batch normalization (BN) and a leak...

area. Therefore, they should be similar in transformation
spaces [46]. Inspired by this observation, we propose a dualbranch CMAFT to extract global contextual knowledge and
simultaneously mine the similarities in the spatial relationship
between the two modalities. Specifically, features Fh and
Fl are first flattened and spliced with an all-zero learnable
classification token to generate sequence features FвЂІh в€€ R N Г—c
and FlвЂІ в€€ R N Г—c , where N = s 2 + 1. Then, learnable position
embeddings are...

where CMAF(В·), LN(В·), and MLP(В·) represent the CMAF,
layer normalization (LN), and multilayer perceptron (MLP)
functions, and Al в€€ Rn h Г—N Г—N denotes the self-attention map
obtained from the first LiDAR branch. Here, n h means the
number of attention heads. The detailed acquisition process of
Al will be explained later.
As a key part of CMAFT, CMAF focuses on discovering
the potential similarities and complementarities between
modalities. To this end, apart from the self-attention within
the mod...

cross-modal attention maps collected from each head space.
Then, KullbackвЂ“Leibler (KL) divergence [50] is introduced to
constrain the correlation between two cross-modal attention
maps. In sum, the correlation loss of D two-branch CMAFTs
can be expressed as
LCOR =

In general, after passing D dual-branch CMAFTs, not only can
the global context of HSI and LiDAR data be exploited, but the
complementary information of the two modalities can also be
deeply mined. This is beneficial for extracting comprehensive
features.
C. AMM Module
For joint classification tasks of multimodal data, learning
imbalance between modalities is a common challenge due to
the heterogeneity of modalities [30]. This imbalance arises
for two main reasons. First, the convergence speeds ...

of the ith sample.

adjust the learning speeds of different modalities by a feature
mask modulation operation. It first converts r into the mask

D. SGFF Module
After obtaining the features zh and zl corresponding HSI and
LiDAR, the next step for the final classification is to fuse them
for the multimodal features. To enhance the discrimination
of the multimodal features, we introduce the SGFF module
in this section. Unlike simple integration, SGFF encourages
each modality to leverage the semantic information from other
modalities to extract multimodal complementary messages
effectively so that the fused multimodal features can be
enhance...

where MМ„h and MМ„l indicate the average of the corresponding
features of the kth class samples calculated in the current
batch, and Оµ is the momentum parameter that is fixed as 0.9 in
this article.

where pi indicates the probability of correct prediction. Considering the correlation loss LCOR mentioned in Section III-B,
the overall loss can be expressed as
L = LCE + О»LCOR

where О» is the balance coefficient, which controls the
importance of the classification loss and correlation loss.
IV. E XPERIMENTS
In this section, we evaluate the performance of the proposed
MICF-Net on three publicly available datasets containing both
HSI and LiDAR data. We assess the modelвЂ™s performance
using three assessment criteria [22]: overall accuracy (OA),
average accuracy (AA), and Kappa coefficient (Kappa). Note
that all experiments are conducted five times, and the average
and stan...

2) MUUFL Dataset [56]: The second dataset was collected
in November 2010 at The University of Southern Mississippi
Gulfport Campus, Long Beach, MS, USA. It comprises
two data sources: HSI and LiDAR data. HSI consists of
325 Г— 220 pixels with 64 bands ranging from 0.38 to 1.05 Вµm.
The LiDAR data were acquired using the Gemini Airborne
Laser Terrain Mapper (ALTM) LiDAR sensor. The number of
labeled pixels in this dataset is 53 687, which are grouped into
11 classes manually. In the following exper...

penalty coefficient О± in AMM, and the momentum parameter
Оµ in AMM. Their empirically optimal values are listed in
Table I, and their impacts on MICF-Net will be presented in
Section IV-E.

C. Classification Performance
To demonstrate the effectiveness of MICF-Net, we select
eight advanced HSI and LiDAR joint classification methods,
including dual attention-based multimodal fusion network
(FusAtNet) [27], adaptive mutual-learning-based multimodal
data fusion network (AM3Net) [58], globalвЂ“local transformer
network (GLT-Net) [59], CALC [45], hierarchical CNN and
transformer (HCT) [60], multimodal fusion transformer (MFT)
[61], ExViT [42], and mixing self-attention and convolution
net...

extraction to capture these details and variations effectively.
However, MICF-Net mainly adopts the transformer structure,
which treats all pixels equally without distinguishing the
geometric complexity of different objects. This manner
may lead to a slight decrease in the generalization ability
of MICF-Net in these specific categories. Fortunately, the
performance gaps between MICF-Net and better methods
in these classes are small, and strengthening the proposed
model for these unacceptable cla...

Pixel-level classification maps of different methods are
depicted in Fig. 7. By observation, we find that some
noise is present in all classification maps. However, MICFNet produces results with more apparent edges and less
noise, which are closest to the ground truth compared
to other models. This superior performance is attributed
to MICF-NetвЂ™s comprehensive exploration of multimodal
complementary information, thereby obtaining more accurate
feature representation.

MICF-Net still outperforms others. For example, in the
classification results of the вЂњRoadвЂќ class, MICF-Net predicts
fewer misclassified pixels and smoother boundaries than
others. Overall, the prediction map of MICF-Net is closest
to the ground truth, ...

3) Number of Dual-Branch CMAFT D: To investigate

reasons behind this are twofold. When there are too few dualbranch CMAFTs, they struggle to capture HSI and LiDARвЂ™s
complex contents adequately. When too many dual-branch
CMAFTs exist, redundant parameters lead to over-fitting on
the training samples, thus hindering generalization. Therefore,
we recommend that D = 2.
4) Middle Layer Size of Project m: In Section III-B,
we fixed m to 8. Here, we change its values in the range of
[2, 16]. The results of MICF-Nets with various m counted on
three da...

upward and then downward, and their optimal values all

5) Balance Coefficient in the Overall Loss О»: The balance
coefficient О» in the overall loss controls the importance of
the correlation term. A larger value of О» implies that the
spatial relationship similarity between the two modalities plays
a more significant role in improving the overall performance.
By changing the values of О» within the range [0.1, 0.9],

modal, resulting in a drastic performance decline. Therefore,

[0.1, 0.9]. The OA values of MICF-Nets are summarized in

F. Feature Visualization
To further verify the effectiveness of the designed MICFNet, we adopt the t-distributed stochastic neighbor embedding
(t-SNE) algorithm [63] to visualize the learned features.
MICF-NetвЂ™s feature distribution maps counted on the Houston,
MUUFL, and Trento datasets are displayed in the second row
of Fig. 12. Also, the feature distribution maps of the bestcompared models (i.e., MACN) are exhibited for reference
and can be found in the first row of Fig. 12. By comparing
the ...

G. Effect of Different Numbers of Training Samples
To evaluate the impact of different numbers of training
samples on the classification performance of MICF-Net,
we vary the number of training samples for each class
from 20 to 100 to train different MICF-Nets. Their OA
curves counted on the three datasets are drawn in Fig. 13.
The results of the compared methods are also counted for
reference. By analyzing these curves, we can see that the
performance of all methods improves as the number of
tra...

and suppressing the strong modality, the feature learning of
weak modalities is effectively promoted. Finally, considering
the heterogeneity and complementarity of multimodal data,
the SGFF module integrates multimodal features into a
discriminative fusion representation. Positive experimental
results on three public HSI and LiDAR joint classification
datasets verify the effectiveness of MICF-Net.

database image with a red box represents the correct matched result.

5. Conclusions
In this paper, a novel dataset for cross-time geo-localization is proposed, named
MTGL40-5. This dataset comprises 40 diverse geographic locations spanning five different
years. For each location, the large-scale original and key images are captured, which contain
the complete and key content of the corresponding location. Subsequently, the key/original
images are split into query/database images for conducting geo-localization. By analyzing
the content difference of the same landmark in different years, we aim to address the crosstime challenge and enhance the practicality of geo-localization. In addition to the traditional
image matching task, we also perform three supplementary tasks, incl...

4.6. Visual Analysis
To visually illustrate the results of the eight compared methods on the MTGL405 dataset, we select two distinct query images as samples. For each query image, we
sort the matching scores of the corresponding database images, and choose the top five
images to save space. The results are shown in Figure 5. Based on the results, all methods
demonstrate a certain capacity for image matching through model training. This is evident

 Remote Sens. 2023, 15, 4229

as the left/right query images contain buildings/water, and most of the matched database
images also contain buildings/water. Furthermore, most of the methods successfully match
the correct top database image. However, certain methods find it difficult to match the
correct database image when some database images exhibit high similarity with the query
image, such as VGG16 with the left query image and FSRA with the right query image.

5.4. Sensitivity Analysis
Three free parameters influence our MIEN-PLA, including the smooth transition
weight, О± (in Equation (5)), the head number of MISA, h (in TAMSFM), and the number of
CIIT encoders, Nt (in TAMSFM). In this section, we deeply study their impacts by changing

paragraphs.text:
5.4. Sensitivity Analysis
Three free parameters influence our MIEN-PLA, including the smooth transition
weight, О± (in Equation (5)), the head number of MISA, h (in TAMSFM), and the number of
CIIT encoders, Nt (in TAMSFM). In this section, we deeply study their impacts by changing
their values individually. First, we fix h = 4, Nt = 1, and vary О± from 0.1 to 0.9 with an
interval of 0.1. In addition, the cases of О± = 0.99 and О± = 0.999 are considered. Next, О± and
Nt are set to be 0.99 and 1, and h...

The results of various MIEN-PLAs with different h counted on three data sets are
shown in Figure 13. For UCM21 and AID30, the behavior of MIEN-PLAs decreases
as h increases. For MWPU45, the performance perturbation of MIEN-PLAs is evident.
Overall, MIEN-PLA performs optimally on different data sets when h equals 2 or 4. This
investigation indicates that MIEN-PLA can learn beneficial messages when the number of
projection spaces is small. When projection spaces are enlarged, the excessive fragmen...

The OA curves of MIEN-PLAs with varied Nt are exhibited in Figure 14. It is easy
to see that MIEN-PLAs become stronger when Nt increases, which implies that the more
CIIT encoders there are, the better the performance of MIEN-PLA. However, the parameter
volume would be increased dramatically with the increase of Nt . In detail, the parameter volume of MIEN-PLAs is 14.60M, 17.75M, and 20.90M when Nt varied from 1 to 3.

5.5. Comparison with Other Classification Models
For RS scene classification with noisy labels, the classification results are decided by the
robustness of the learning algorithm and the ability of the feature extractor. In Section 5.2,
we demonstrated the effectiveness of our PLA noise tolerance learning algorithm by only
using ResNet18 as the backbone of MIEN (i.e., Res-PLA). In this section, we choose several
networks designed for RS scenes as the backbones of the classification and teacher s...

A, and MIEN-R, respectively. The above models are trained using PLA, and the backbonesвЂ™

under the scenario of NR = 0.1 are summarized in Table 7. In addition, Giga floating point
operations (GFLOPs) values of different MIENs are provided for reference.

By observing the results, we can see that our MIEN achieves the best OA values
on UCM21 and the second-best OA values on AID30 and NWPU45. This proves the

room for improvement when facing large-scale complex RS scene data sets such as AID30
and NWPU45. Although our MIEN is not optimal in OA, its GFLOPs are the best, showing

6. Discussion
In this section, we further analyze the differences, advantages, disadvantages, and future directions of our MIEN-PLA in comparison to similar methods.
6.1. Similar Work Comparison
In recent years, noisy RS scene classification has been a hot research topic, and many
successful methods have been proposed [50вЂ“54,67,73вЂ“75]. Among them, CMR-NLD [50],
RS-COCL [54], and NLNL [67] are similar to our methods as they are also aimed at reducing
the impact of noisy labels on the model from d...

other methods can be summarized as follows. CMR-NLD, RS-COCL, and NLNL focus on
filtering out noisy scenes during training, whereas our methods are aimed at discovering
the possible noisy data and relabeling them. This way, all samples can be used to train the
model, preventing information loss. Meanwhile, compared to two RS-oriented methods
(CMR-NLD and RS-COCL), which only use the existing CNNs to generate the visual
features, our methods develop a specific network (i.e., MIEN) to explore the ...
